weaknesses.name: Improper Input Validation
weaknesses.description: The product receives input or data, but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the input has the properties that are required to process the data safely and correctly.
weaknesses.extended_description: Input validation is a frequently-used technique for checking potentially dangerous inputs in order to ensure that the inputs are safe for processing within the code, or when communicating with other components. When software does not validate input properly, an attacker is able to craft the input in a form that is not expected by the rest of the application.

detection_methods.description: This weakness can often be detected using automated static analysis tools.
Fuzzing can be useful for identifying unexpected inputs that lead to crashes or unexpected behavior.

mitigations.description: Assume all input is malicious. Use an 'accept known good' input validation strategy.
Use libraries or frameworks that make it easier to generate properly constructed output.

weaknesses.name: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
weaknesses.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that is intended to identify a file or directory that is located underneath a restricted parent directory, but the product does not properly neutralize special elements within the pathname that can cause the pathname to resolve to a location that is outside of the restricted directory.
weaknesses.extended_description: Many file operations are intended to take place within a restricted directory. By using special elements such as '..' and '/' separators, attackers can escape out of the restricted location to access files or directories that are elsewhere on the system.

detection_methods.description: Automated static analysis tools can detect path traversal issues.
Manual review of filesystem access code.

mitigations.description: Use canonicalization to resolve symbolic links, '..', and '.' before performing file operations.
Use a sandbox or restricted environment that enforces strict boundaries on filesystem access.

weaknesses.name: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
weaknesses.description: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes user-controllable input before it is placed in output that is used as a web page that is served to other users.
weaknesses.extended_description: Cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when: 1. Untrusted data enters a web application, typically from a web request. 2. The web application dynamically generates a web page that contains this untrusted data. 3. During page generation, the application does not prevent the data from containing content that is executable by a web browser.

detection_methods.description: Static analysis tools can identify reflected XSS but may miss stored or DOM-based variants.
Web application vulnerability scanners can detect XSS automatically.

mitigations.description: Use a vetted library or framework that does not allow this weakness to occur or provides constructs that make this weakness easier to avoid.
Understand the context in which your data will be used and the encoding that will be expected.
Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) to restrict where scripts can be loaded from.

weaknesses.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')
weaknesses.description: The product constructs all or part of an SQL command using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the intended SQL command when it is sent to a downstream component.
weaknesses.extended_description: Without sufficient removal or quoting of SQL syntax in user-controllable inputs, the generated SQL query can cause those inputs to be interpreted as SQL instead of ordinary user data. This can be used to alter query logic to bypass security checks, or to insert additional statements that modify the back-end database.

detection_methods.description: This weakness can be detected using automated taint-tracking analysis.
SQL injection scanners can detect common injection points.

mitigations.description: Use parameterized queries (prepared statements) for all database queries.
If you need to use dynamic queries, quote arguments and escape special characters specific to the SQL dialect being used.

weaknesses.name: Out-of-bounds Read
weaknesses.description: The product reads data past the end, or before the beginning, of the intended buffer.
weaknesses.extended_description: By reading out-of-bounds memory, an attacker might be able to get secret values, such as memory addresses, which can be bypass protection mechanisms such as ASLR in order to improve the reliability and likelihood of exploiting a separate weakness to achieve code execution instead of just denial of service.

common_consequences.note: Can expose sensitive memory contents (e.g. Heartbleed).

detection_methods.description: Fuzzing can expose out-of-bounds reads through unexpected crashes.
Static analysis with memory safety tools (e.g. AddressSanitizer).

mitigations.description: Use a language that provides appropriate memory abstraction.
Check buffer boundaries if operating on the buffer in a loop.

weaknesses.name: Improper Authentication
weaknesses.description: When an actor claims to have a given identity, the product does not prove or insufficiently proves that the claim is correct.
weaknesses.extended_description: Authentication weaknesses allow unauthenticated actors to access resources that should be restricted. This can range from completely bypassing authentication to more subtle flaws that allow authentication to be circumvented under specific conditions.

detection_methods.description: Review authentication flows for logical flaws.
Some static tools can identify missing or weak authentication checks.

mitigations.description: Divide the system into components and enforce trust boundaries between them.
Use a multi-factor authentication mechanism.

weaknesses.name: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')
weaknesses.description: The product contains a code sequence that can run concurrently with other code, and the code sequence requires temporary, exclusive access to a shared resource, but a timing window exists in which the shared resource can be modified by another code sequence that is operating concurrently.
weaknesses.extended_description: Race conditions can occur when two threads or processes share a resource and the outcome of the program depends on the sequence of execution. This can lead to unpredictable behavior, data corruption, or security vulnerabilities.

detection_methods.description: Concurrency analysis tools can detect race conditions.

detection_methods.method: Dynamic Analysis with Manual Results Interpretation
detection_methods.description: Running the program under stress conditions may expose race conditions.

mitigations.description: Use synchronization primitives such as mutexes or semaphores to control access to shared resources.
Use thread-safe data structures and APIs.

weaknesses.name: Deserialization of Untrusted Data
weaknesses.description: The product deserializes untrusted data without sufficiently verifying that the resulting data will be valid.
weaknesses.extended_description: It is often convenient to serialize objects for communication or to save them for later use. However, deserialization of data from an untrusted source can result in security problems such as arbitrary code execution, denial of service attacks, and authentication bypass.

common_consequences.note: Arbitrary code execution via gadget chains is common.

detection_methods.description: Static analysis can identify use of deserialization libraries on untrusted data.

mitigations.description: Avoid deserializing data from untrusted sources.
Use integrity checks such as digital signatures to verify serialized data before deserialization.

weaknesses.name: Externally Controlled Reference to a Resource in Another Sphere
weaknesses.description: The product uses an externally controlled name or reference that resolves to a resource that is outside of the intended control sphere.
weaknesses.extended_description: This weakness covers a variety of techniques for attacker-controlled references to external resources, including URL redirection, remote file inclusion, and external entity injection. The attacker can influence or completely control the resource that the product accesses.

detection_methods.description: Taint analysis can identify externally controlled references.

mitigations.description: Avoid allowing user-controllable data to directly reference external resources.
Validate and canonicalize any externally supplied resource identifiers.

weaknesses.name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
weaknesses.description: The web server receives a URL or similar request from an upstream component and retrieves the contents of this URL, but it does not sufficiently ensure that the request is being sent to the expected destination.
weaknesses.extended_description: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) occurs when an attacker can cause the server to make requests to an unintended location. The attacker may be able to reach internal services behind firewalls, access cloud metadata services, or pivot to other internal systems.

common_consequences.note: Internal service endpoints and cloud metadata can be accessed.

detection_methods.description: Taint analysis can identify externally controlled URLs used in server-side requests.
Scanning for SSRF by probing internal network addresses.

mitigations.description: Use allowlists of acceptable URLs or IP ranges for outbound connections.
Validate and sanitize all user-supplied URLs. Deny requests to private, loopback, and link-local address ranges.
